lines.text: #15 FEB. 6, 1953. BRIDGEPORT, CONN. 41 1/2°, 73 1/4°. A large explosion and [unclear] rocked Connecticut, on 2 separate days, one accompanied with a flash of light.

#16 FEB. 8, 1953. 1:10 AM. RUSAPE, SOUTHERN RHODESIA. A brilliant multi-colored flash of light was seen, and a swishing sound was heard when a warm, hollow metal cylinder landed on ground. The object was examined by a Dr. Graham White of a British Rocket society, but details on object never came out. Believed to be part of a weather balloon apparatus.

#17 FEB. 8 1953. 10:20 AM. BULAWAYO, SOUTHERN RHODESIA. 20°, 28°. Four saucers at a 700 foot altitude crossed sky. (COMMENT: This was only a few miles from the Rusape area where the unknown metal ball fell.)

#18 FEB. 9 1953. Night. FRANKLIN, VIRGINIA. 36 1/2°, 77°. A circular silvery object about 35 feet in diameter, with windows hovered in sky for 2 nights. The windows appeared blue-lighted, while object gave off a red light. It was supposedly chased by jets, which it quickly outran. (COMMENT: Saucers with windows are rare, but the colors of this object appear quite correct.)

#19 FEB. 9 1953. Night. Near CHERRY POINT, NORTH CAROLINA. A silver saucer was seen from the ground. A marine pilot flew about 30 minutes trying to find it, then saw 2 red lights like plane lights move vertically 10,000 feet up in a few seconds. He raced toward object at 500 mph, saw a white hot object with 2 red side lights about 10 miles away, before it disappeared in a flash of light when speeding away. (COMMENT: The sudden disappearance in a 'lightflash' is similar to many saucer report, where saucer speeds up so fast as to seem'invisib

#20 FEB. 10 1953. Morning. TUJUNGA, CALIFORNIA. (Near L.A.) A saucer like object emitting smoke and flames appeared to change shape from a saucer to a Ball to a cigar, as it hovered in sky. (COMMENT: This is a rare report, similar to one over Richlands, Washington in July 1952. It might be explained by the saucer showing different sides at intervals
#21 FEB. 11 1953. Night. MARION, SOUTH CAROLINA. 34 1/8°, 80 0/8°. Residents observed a flying saucer.
#22 FEB. 11 1953. Night. MYRTLE BEACH, SOUTH CAROLINA. 33 3/4°, 78 3/4°. [unclear] crossed sky. This may have been same object as Marion report, but no details are available.

#23 FEB. 11 1953. Evening. SURF CITY, NEW JERSEY. 39 2/3°, 74 1/3°. A huge blue white or reddish ball of fire appeared to fall into sea after it noiselessly crossed sky. (COMMENT: This description fits a meteor, but report included only because of 2 reports the same night over N. C.
#24 FEB. 12 1953. 8:15 P.M. WASHINGTON, DC. 39°, 77°. A saucer was watched at nearby Kensington for a short time. No other details from report on radio.
#25 FEB. 12 1953. Night. POINT ARENA, CALIFORNIA. 39°, 123 3/4°. A bright white light observed in sky. No other details given, and this may be a meteor.

#26 FEB. 12 1953. Night. PALESTINE, TEXAS. 31 3/4°, 95 1/2°. A flying object carrying one red and one green light observed by engineer through binoculars. Local radio station also besieged by many local observers.
